docs.title: Switch statement in assembly using Jump Table
docs.text:
switch (k) {
	case 0: f = i + j;
		break;
	case 1: f = g + h;
		break;
	case 2: f = g - h;
		break;
	case 3: f = i - j;
		break;
}

f: $s0
j: $s1
h: $s2
i: $s3
j: $s4
k: $s5

.data
jTable: .word L0, L1, L2, L3, Exit

.text

sll $s5, $s5, 2
la $t0, jTable
add $s5, $s5, $t0
jr $s5
L0: add $s0, $s1, $s2
j Exit
L1: add
j Exit
L2: sub
j Exit
L3: sub
j Exit
Exit:

docs.title: How do I Study for this Course?
docs.text:
• Practice, practice, practice
  • Attend recitations/labs (again, practice, practice, practice!)
  • Come to class, ask questions!

docs.title: Resources
docs.text: This is a list of helpful resources for learning CDA 3103C content, contributed to by many students and Professor Aedo, compiled by sh0inx.

docs.title: How to Use This
docs.text:
Generally speaking, you want to use these as supplemental resources to the textbook and class notes. While the textbook is not required, it is optional, and provides much of the background and context in which the class is taught.

Get a good night’s rest, hit the books when you’re ready. If you’re unsure how you want to study, here are some helpful tips, but most importantly, do what works for you and doesn’t make you hate the material.

docs.title: Guide 1   Setting up Your Compiler
docs.text:
📌 PART 1: C DEVELOPMENT ENVIRONMENT (WSL + LINUX)

To program in C for CDA3103C, you’ll need a Linux environment.

1️⃣ Install WSL (Windows Subsystem for Linux)

  • Open PowerShell as Administrator
  • Run:
wsl --install
  • Restart your computer
  • Complete the Ubuntu setup (username + password)

2️⃣ Install Build Tools (Compiler + Debugger) Open your Ubuntu terminal and run:

sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade -y
sudo apt install build-essential gdb -y

3️⃣ Install VS Code + Extensions

  • Install VS Code

  • Add extensions:

  • ✅ WSL Extension

  • ✅ C/C++ Extension Pack

4️⃣ Start Coding 🚀

  • Open your Ubuntu terminal
  • Navigate to your folder
  • Run:
code .
  • This launches VS Code connected to Linux

💡 Tip: Always code inside WSL (Linux), not Windows, for this class.

docs.title: Guide 2   Github and Security
docs.text:
📌 PART 2: GITHUB + SECURITY SETUP

1️⃣ Get GitHub Student Pack 🎓

  • Go to: https://education.github.com/pack
  • Sign up using your your-NID@ucf.edu** email

2️⃣ Configure Git Identity (IMPORTANT) In your WSL terminal:

git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "your-NID@ucf.edu"

3️⃣ Generate SSH Key 🔑 Run:

ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "your-NID@ucf.edu"

Press Enter through prompts.

4️⃣ Add SSH Key to GitHub

  • Show your key:
cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub
  • Copy the output
  • Go to GitHub → Settings → SSH Keys → New Key
  • Paste and save

💡 Why SSH? No passwords needed when pushing code—faster and more secure.

docs.title: Guide 3   Collaboration (Team Workflow)
docs.text:
📌 PART 3: TEAMWORK + GIT WORKFLOW

1️⃣ Project Setup

  • Team leader creates a GitHub repo
  • Invite teammates as collaborators
  • Teammates clone the repo:
git clone <repo-url>

2️⃣ Create a .gitignore File 🚫 Inside your repo, create .gitignore and add:

*.o
a.out
.vscode/

This prevents unnecessary files from being uploaded.

3️⃣ Basic Workflow (VERY IMPORTANT)

✔ Always follow this order:

  1. Pull latest changes:
git pull

  1. Make your changes

  2. Commit your work:

git add .
git commit -m "Describe your changes"
  1. Push to GitHub:
git push

⚠️ Golden Rule: 👉 ALWAYS pull before you start working (prevents conflicts and broken code)

💡 Tip: Use VS Code’s Source Control tab if you prefer a UI instead of commands.
